## [03:01 - 06:02]

Sometimes they're qualifying too hard. This could be kind of one of two things. The sales rep is only making offers to prospects. They know they can close. Okay, so this one's based out of ego here. This one often happens when I've seen this happen before, when let's say the CEO or the owner came down on the sales rep for a bad, somebody who's bad that they enrolled and they ended up having to refund them. So the sales rep feels really bad about it. They're like, oh man, I enrolled the wrong person. They had to get refunded. So they lost all of their certainty and then they start qualifying and tightening down the valve super hard. So then they're only making offers to people who are like beyond a fit, right? And then your offer percentage goes down, your revenue goes down. Because the simple math is the more offers that you make by and large as a company, the more money you're gonna make. That's just how it works. So the other thing with this is that a sales rep's doing too many two-call closes and indoor delays, okay? This might not be an issue in your company, but I've seen it before when I've teach reps how to two-call close and delay. They pendulum swing and they didn't wanna do this to everybody. Well, the metric that's gonna show up at is low offer percentage. So you wanna dig in and see if that's what's going on. Also, very, very possible that the marketing quality could drop. So if they're on the phone with people who are on a fit, then they're on the phone with people who aren't on a fit. That's a marketing issue. So solutions to this. First, bring attention to it and let them self-diagnose. Cause if you have a smart driven individual, they're gonna be able to tell you what's going on. When in doubt, encourage them to be aggressive with making offers and assure them if they make a back call, they don't enroll the right person, no worries. They did the right thing. You'll make it right, you'll refund them and there won't be any reprimanding. This is a really good piece of advice.
